text: to establish sociology as an empirically founded science: state statistics on suicide and ethnography of primitive religion. Even here the very same mediating role of Mauss in the data-gathering is striking: Mauss had learned much about aboriginal religion and totemism by studying it with van Gennep (and by reading his work); ten years earlier it was again Mauss who directed himself to Tarde who as ‘Director of the Bureau of Legal Statistics’ would grant Mauss and Durkheim access to the statistical material that made up the entire empirical dataset used for Suicide. Third, and as already hinted at, in terms of methodological approach, van Gennep and Tarde come quite close to each other. This also concerns the relationship to neighbouring disciplines of ethnography and sociology. Durkheim’s own position is ambivalent: on the one hand he wants to borrow and implement the scientific method from the natural sciences; on the other hand he wants sociology to cut its ties completely from biology and physics. Tarde’s position is the reverse (Latour 2010: 3): ‘for him there exist only societies. Human societies are but a particular subset of these societies because they exist in so few copies. But since human societies are accessible through their most intimate features, social scientists have no need to let natural scientists dictate what their epistemology should be’. As Tarde put it himself (1903: 1): ‘In social subjects we are exceptionally privileged in having veritable causes, positive and specific acts; this condition is wholly lacking in every other subject of investigation’. This coheres perfectly with van Gennep’s approach. Van Gennep insists on the irreducibility of social phenomena, but at the same time he refuses to cut off the social sciences from what he at times called the ‘great tree of life’. 19 Just like Tarde, van Gennep based 19 And here again, on this crucial point of methodology, Mauss would move closer to both Tarde and van Gennep after Durkheim’s death, which is most visible in his 1927 discussion of the new divisions of the social sciences; here Mauss pays lip service to Durkheim’s categorical divisions, then slowly but surely moves beyond them in both form and substance, and ends on the following statement: ‘Normally the greatest advances occur on the borders between the division of sociology, as on the borders between all sciences and parts of sciences’ (Mauss 2005[1927]: 65, my emphasis). Liminality and the Modern 70 his interpretations on the assumption that man is a part of nature and is therefore subject to the great natural laws of stability, variability, and change. Fourth, both Tarde and van Gennep concurred that Durkheim and his followers simply failed to capture exactly what they themselves argued that sociology and anthropology should be all about, namely the collective level and ‘collective representations’. Where do such representations come from? How are they established? How are they passed on? How do they interrelate with the individual? The parallels between the Tarde/Durkheim and the Durkheim/Arnold van Gennep debates are both strong and important here. Both thinkers were equally aware of the problematic dismissal of historical individuals as agents of society and social change. Both Gabriel Tarde and Arnold van Gennep saw the extent to which it was Durkheim himself, against all his proclamations, who had turned society into a ‘metaphysical entity’. 20 While the closeness of these parallels may be open to further discussion, the real point to stress in this context does not simply belong to intellectual history. The central concept that Tarde engaged was that of imitation. In Chapter 4 of Suicide Durkheim violently and categorically dismissed the relevance of this concept for the social sciences (see again Thomassen 2012d). 21 Even though Durkheim’s argument is an evident absurdity (have we ever lived in a more imitative age?), this was also a debate he ‘won’. No introductory books on sociology list imitation as a key concept even today. Likewise, van Gennep’s recognition of the centrality of liminality is only now, with a century of delay, starting to reach us. Liminality and imitation belong together in our attempt to formulate an analytical platform for approaching change and transition and human reactions to in-between periods and situations. However, this exercise must pay due attention to the work of an anthropologist who did more than any other social scientist to re-launch Arnold van Gennep and the concept of liminality: Victor Turner. 20 Incidentally, Durkheim, even during his studies at the École Normale, was soon nicknamed ‘the metaphysician’ by his peers. 21 Tarde wrote a prompt answer to Durkheim in which he rebuts the worst of Durkheim’s claims, but for reasons we can only guess at chose not to publish them. Tarde’s hand-written notes were recently transcribed and published by Besnard and Berlandi (Tarde 2000). Chapter 3 Liminality Rediscovered: With Victor Turner and Beyond This chapter sets itself a simple task: to trace the reception of Van Gennep’s ideas and how they were developed in the latter part of the twentieth century and up until today, with a focus on the concept of liminality. This is where we have to go in some detail into the work of Victor Turner, and for obvious reasons: it was via the work of Turner that liminality re-entered anthropology and the social sciences. Both the merits and limits of Turner’s work will be discussed. Arnold van Gennep: Delayed Reception and Posthumous Recognition Van Gennep became known in Anglo-Saxon anthropology after 1960 with the translation of Rites of Passage. This happened in the context of British anthropologists’ renewed interests in theoretical developments within French anthropology. Rodney Needham and Edmund Leach, supported by Evans-Pritchard, took the initiative to translate the most important classics of French anthropologists, like Marcel Mauss, Robert Hertz, Hubert and Mauss, and indeed also of some of Durkheim’s less well-known works. It was also Needham who (somewhat surprisingly perhaps, given the marginality of the book within van Gennep’s own oeuvre) translated and introduced van Gennep’s otherwise little known The Semi-Scholars in 1967.
